Q1: Honestly, my study routine was all over the place. I'd have these bursts where I'd study intensely for hours, usually late at night when I could actually focus, and then nothing for days. Being a kinesthetic learner, I needed to physically do things to understand them, so I'd often be working on practice problems or building things rather than just reading. With limited resources at home, I had to plan around when I could access certain materials or spaces. I tried to organize myself but life kept getting in the way.
Q2: My pattern was definitely chaotic and deadline-driven. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything, and then suddenly I'd realize something was due and cram everything in. I have extracurricular commitments that eat into my time unpredictably, so I couldn't really establish a consistent weekly schedule. The stress of not knowing when I'd have time made me feel scared a lot of the time. I'd tell myself I'd study on certain days but it rarely worked out that way.
Q3: Usually panic, if I'm being honest. I'd log in when I suddenly remembered an assignment or when someone in my group chat mentioned something was due. Sometimes the gamified tasks would send notifications that made me curious enough to check in. Fear of missing something important was probably my main motivator. I rarely logged in just to browse or explore the content proactively.
Q4: Not really, no. I never got into a comfortable rhythm with the LMS. The gamified elements helped a bit because they made it feel less like a chore and more like checking a game, but even that didn't become habitual. I'd go through phases where I'd log in more frequently, but then life would happen and I'd disappear for a while. It always felt like something I had to force myself to do rather than something natural.
Q5: The hands-on activities and anything interactive were most useful to me because that's how my brain works. I learn by doing, not by reading or watching. The gamified tasks actually helped because they gave me immediate feedback and made me feel like I was accomplishing something. Discussion posts from peers were occasionally helpful when they explained concepts in different ways. Videos were okay but only if I could pause and try things myself.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and longer videos. They just didn't stick for me no matter how many times I tried. The discussion forums felt pointless most of the time because by the time I logged in, the conversation had moved on. I delayed a lot of the optional content because I was always playing catch-up on the required stuff. With my limited resources, I had to prioritize what I thought would directly impact my grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it connected to something practical I could use. The gamified elements helped with this because I could see my progress and earn something for my effort. If something felt too abstract or theoretical, I'd lose interest quickly. I needed to feel like I was building toward something tangible, not just consuming information passively.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. When you're already struggling to keep up with required work and dealing with stress, optional stuff just falls off the radar completely. Required activities had that fear factor attached to them, so I'd eventually do them even if I procrastinated. Optional ones never had that urgency, so they'd sit there untouched. The gamified optional tasks were slightly more appealing but still not enough to prioritize.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my real learning happened offline. I'd download materials and work through them on my own time, away from the computer. I'd practice things physically, write notes by hand, work through problems on paper. I also used external YouTube tutorials and free resources because sometimes they explained things better. My LMS activity probably looked minimal compared to how much I was actually studying.
Q10: There were so many times like this. I'd download a PDF and spend hours working through it with handwritten notes, but the LMS would only show that I clicked on it once. I'd watch a concept video once online, then replay it in my head while practicing physically. Group study sessions with classmates where we'd work through problems together wouldn't show up anywhere. My best learning happened away from screens.
Q11: Yes, constantly. With my internet situation not always being reliable and limited resources, I learned to download everything I could in one go. I'd access a module once, save everything locally, and then work through it over several days offline. The LMS would show one quick login, but I'd actually spend hours with that material later. It was more efficient for me to work that way.
Q12: There were definitely dark periods where everything felt overwhelming. When my stress peaked and I felt scared about falling behind, I'd actually freeze up and avoid the course entirely. Financial pressures and my extracurricular commitments would sometimes collide with coursework, and the course would lose. Mid-semester was particularly rough when everything piled up. My low resilience meant I didn't bounce back quickly from setbacks.
Q13: All of those things affected me constantly. The workload felt crushing at times, especially when combined with my other responsibilities. Confusion made me avoid certain topics because I didn't know where to start. Fatigue from trying to balance everything meant I'd log in but just stare at the screen without absorbing anything. My competing responsibilities always seemed more urgent than coursework, even when they weren't.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open materials and scroll through them without really processing anything. Sometimes I'd have a video playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, worrying about other things. The fear and stress I carried made it hard to actually focus even when I was technically present. I'd finish a reading and realize I couldn't remember a single thing from it.
Q15: I avoided assignments constantly, even important ones. The fear of not doing them well enough made me put them off until the last possible moment. Some tasks felt so overwhelming that starting them seemed impossible. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with the same excuse. My low resilience meant that once I fell behind, catching up felt insurmountable.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage with the LMS. I'd scramble in the final hours before something was due, which is probably why my assignment completion rate was so low. The panic would set in and suddenly I'd be very active, but it was chaotic and stressful. Without deadlines, I probably wouldn't have engaged at all. They were both my enemy and my only motivator.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it did affect me, but I often didn't check it in time for it to matter. Positive feedback gave me a temporary boost, but negative feedback would make me spiral and avoid the course for a while. The gamified tasks gave more immediate feedback which was helpful because I could adjust quickly. But formal feedback on assignments often came too late to change my approach.
Q18: The course structure felt rigid in ways that didn't work for my learning style. I needed more hands-on, practical components and fewer passive readings. The flexibility of online courses should have helped me, but instead it just gave me more rope to hang myself with through procrastination. The gamified elements were a nice touch but weren't enough to overcome the overall structure issues for someone like me.
Q19: The gamified tasks made engagement easier because they broke things into smaller, achievable chunks. But the heavy reliance on reading and video content made it harder for me as a kinesthetic learner. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't match how I naturally learn. Having more practical, hands-on assessments would have helped me engage better. The asynchronous nature was both a blessing and a curse.
Q20: I think those students might be like me, searching for something that makes sense to them but not finding it. They might be opening everything trying to understand but struggling to complete the actual work. Or they could be dealing with personal issues that make finishing things impossible even when they're trying. High activity without submissions could also mean perfectionism or fear of failure. Sometimes you're present but paralyzed.
Q21: That actually sounds like me to some extent. My exam scores and assignment quality were decent when I actually submitted things, even though my LMS activity was low. I did a lot of learning offline that didn't show up in the system. Some people are just efficient learners who don't need to engage with every resource. Or they have external resources and study methods that work better for them than the LMS.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in and what they click on, but they miss everything that happens offline. They can't measure understanding or genuine engagement with material. They might show someone watching a video but not whether they were actually paying attention. The emotional state behind the numbers is completely invisible. My scared feelings and stress never showed up in any log.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just going through the motions but genuinely trying to understand and apply things. For me, engagement happens when I can physically interact with concepts, not just read about them. It means showing up mentally, not just logging in. Real engagement should feel like growth, not just completion.
Q24: So much wouldn't show up. My fear and stress that affected everything I did. The hours I spent studying offline with downloaded materials. The group study sessions with classmates. My genuine interest in learning even when I couldn't engage with the platform consistently. The personal struggles with resources and competing responsibilities that shaped my participation. The real me behind the numbers is way more complicated than any data could capture.
